mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the Aztec state, its military campaigns under Itzcoatl and Montezuma, and Aztec culture and society up to the Spanish conquest (C.2-C.3).
Directly covers Maya civilization in Classic and Post-Classic periods, including urban settlements, temple-pyramids, the calendar, writing system, and the collapse (B.2, C.1).
Narrates the Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire and the broader European subjugation of Meso-America that ended the Post-Classic Period (C.2-C.3).
Introduces the excavation and dating methods essential for studying Olmec, Teotihuacan, Maya, and Aztec sites, including ceremonial architecture and pottery analysis (A, B.1-B.2).
Traces Late Pleistocene migrations and Early Hunter peoples through the emergence of agriculture around 6500 BC in Meso-America (B.1.a).
Provides ethnographic frameworks for understanding Meso-American societies (A), Aztec political organization, religious ritual, and social hierarchies (C.3).
Examines documentary sources about Meso-American civilizations, including chronicles by Cortes, Bernal Diaz del Castillo, and Bartolome de Las Casas (A).
Explores mythological traditions such as the legend of Quetzalcoatl (C.1), Maya cosmology (B.2.c.ii), and Aztec sacrificial religion (C.3).
Situates Meso-American peoples within the broader world of indigenous cultures of the Americas, providing ethnographic context for pre-Columbian societies (A).
